dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4, 15-year-old male conscious with reported drug ingestion at a school in Richmond. Overdose/Poisoning call, respond immediately.
Hello, I'm EMT Jake with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what's going on here?
The school staff reported that he was confused and taking pills. He said he took some of his mom's promethazine to get high.
Ma'am, can you tell me if the patient recognizes his name and responds appropriately?
I shouted his name, and he slowly responded. He was very slow to respond, but he understood what I said.
Can I check his eye opening now using a light source for assessment? Do you mind?
He followed the light and blinked. Now we're going to test motor response. Can I get assistance?
Hello, I'm EMT Jake. Can we perform a quick check of your airway, breathing, and circulation? Would that be okay with you?
Airway is patent and open. No signs of obstruction. Good.
Eyes are approximately four millimeters and appear PERRL.
Hi, what is your name?
Nick.
Can you squeeze my fingers? Do you know where you are?
I am at school. I got sleepy from taking some meds.
Can you tell me where we are right now?
At school, I think? I don't really know.
What is today's date?
September, 2019. Not sure exactly what day it is though.
Hi, I'm going to check your level of responsiveness with the Glasgow Coma Scale now. Can I ask a few more questions?
Eyes are 4mm and PERRL, motor response is intact. GCS is 15 (Eye = 4, Verbal = 5, Motor = 6).
All right, he is currently alert. The Glasgow Coma Scale is 15 (Eye = 4, Verbal = 5, Motor = 6). He is A&O x4.
Let me take a good look at his breathing. Can I have a partner to time the rate?
Respirations are at a rate of 15 with regular effort. Effort and depth are regular. They are bilaterally clear.
SpO2 is at 96%. Looks good. What's the next vital?
Breathing is consistent. It is controlled with regular depth. No signs of labored breathing overall.
Comfortable in a semi-upright position, with no abnormal respiratory sounds. BBS are clear and equal bilaterally. Let's move on to circulation.
Radial pulses are strong, equal, and regular. He shows no signs of distress in the extremities.
Pulse is at 116, strong. We should do a full assessment of his circulation.
BP is 135 over 80.
The mucus membranes are dry. Skin is pale and dry, and warm to the touch. No significant drainage or bleeding.
Blood glucose is at 0. This is a critical finding. Any history of hypoglycemic risk?
No prior medical history of hypoglycemia. We will closely monitor glucose levels.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia.
No signs of general skin trauma, petechiae, or rash.
Eyes are 4mm and PERRL. Head and neck are intact with no injury signs.
Lips are slightly bluish. No visible oral sores or obstructions.
Let's do a chest inspection. Are there any signs of respiratory distress?
On visual inspection, the chest movement is normal. Bilateral breath sounds are clear and equal.
Bilateral breath sounds are clear upon auscultation. No adventitious sounds noted. Next step, upper extremities.
Upper extremities appear normal. No signs of asymmetry noted.
PMS positive bilaterally. No deformities or swelling detected. Next, lower extremities.
Normal gait with no signs of limping or visible pain. No signs of edema or signs of DVT.
We are going to go over your symptoms and history briefly. Can you tell me about your current symptoms?
I have no pain, but sort of sleepy. Otherwise, fine. No nausea or vomiting.
Any allergies you want to mention?
No known allergies.
Are you on any regular medications other than the ones just mentioned?
No, I don't take anything regularly.
What led up to the event? Can you tell me when and how it started?
I just took whatever was in my mom's cabinet. I took 8-10 pills today.
On a scale of 0 to 10 where 10 is the worst you've felt, would you say any part of your body is painful?
I feel a little sleepy but not in pain. I would say 0 maybe, maybe 1 or 2 at most.
We are now going to start an IV. Do you mind?
The IV started at the left arm halfway and secured with tape after inserting a 20-gauge needle into the vein.
IV was successful after one attempt. We're going to use it as primary IV access.
We will now place leads on you for cardiac monitoring.
Leads positioned. Four-lead ECG is now active.
What protocol fits this case for overdose?
According to current medical findings, Protocol 9-9 for Medical - Overdose/Poisoning/Toxic Ingestion.
Let me take another reading now. Ready.
Respirations are at 18. Rhythm is regular.
Pulse has decreased slightly to 111.
Blood pressure is at 123/71. It has dropped a bit.
Glucose is still at 0.
SpO2 is at 95%. A slight decrease but still in the acceptable range.
Where is the best hospital for transfer in this situation?
Chippenham Hospital Cold is nearby and best suited.
We are placing the patient on the stretcher in a Semi-Fowler position.
He's secured with a shoulder and lap belt. Ready for transport.
Let me check again after positioning.
Pulse is at 106. It's trending lower.
Respirations are back to 15.
Blood pressure is now at 125/81.
Glucose is still at 0. No signs of hypoglycemic symptoms for now.
SpO2 is 98%. Improved a little.
We're ready to transport him to the Pediatric Emergency Room at Chippenham Hospital.
The patient is secured on the stretcher in Semi-Fowler's position.
He is breathing well. No signs of distress.
Checking IV again for any signs of infiltration.
IV line looks clear.
etCO2 is showing consistent waveform with adequate breathing.
He is alert. Possible changes may come, but not now.
We will be arriving shortly. Please prepare room 4 for this 15-year-old.
Still at 0 for pain? Confirmed.
Let me just check respiratory status again while in transit.
Breathing is regular and shows no sign of distress.
All procedures are in progress. Arrival in approximately 3 minutes.
Thank you for the cooperation. Ready for transport.
